standard_description:
Understand a fraction a/b with a > 1 as a sum of fractions 1/b.
Grade 4 expectations in this domain are limited to fractions with denominators 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 100.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Problem 1
Solve. Show or explain your work.
$${2{5\over8}+4{2\over3}}$$
Problem 2
Sang needs
$$3\tfrac{1}{2}$$
feet of string to make a necklace and
$$2\tfrac{1}{4}$$
feet of string left. How much string, in feet, did Sang have before making the necklace?

Problem 1
32 students shared 6 pizzas equally. How much pizza will each student get?
a.   Find the exact answer.
b.   Check your work.
Problem 2
32 gallons of water is used to completely fill 6 fish tanks. If each tank holds the same amount of water, how many gallons will each tank hold?
a.   Find the exact answer. Write it as a fraction and as a mixed number.
b.   Determine which two whole numbers the answer is in between.
c.   Explain how the fractional part of the mixed number is related to the remainder in the problem.
d.   Check your work.
Problem 3
Solve the following problems. Think about the main differences between each one.
a.   A teacher has 21 batteries. Each calculator uses 4 batteries. How many calculators can the teacher fill with batteries?
b.   Four children can ride in a car. How many cars are needed to take 21 children to the museum?
c.   Ms. Cole bakes 21 cookies and wants to share them out fairly to 4 friends. How many cookies will each friend get?

Problem 1
a.   Ms. Needham made a lasagna for dinner with her family. She cut the lasagna into 12 pieces. Her family ate 4 pieces of lasagna the night that she made it. The next day, they ate 3 more pieces. What fraction of the lasagna did Ms. Needham’s family eat altogether?
b.   Ms. Roll is stringing beads to make a 16-inch necklace. At the end of the first night of working on it, she has 5 inches of beads strung. At the end of the second night of working on it, she has another 7 inches of beads strung. What fraction of the necklace has Ms. Roll completed?
Problem 2
Solve. Show your work with an area model or a number line.
a.
$$\frac{7}{8}+\frac{6}{8}$$
b.
$$\frac{13}{12}+\frac{5}{12}$$
Problem 3
Solve. Show or explain your work.
a.
$${5{1\over8}+{5\over8}}$$
b.
$${9{11\over12}+{11\over12}}$$
c.
$${3{4\over17}+4{8\over17}}$$
d.
$${2{11\over15}+2{13\over15}}$$

Problem 1
__________ graham crackers are shared equally by __________ people.
a.   Choose numbers to fill in the blanks. You can only use each number once: 2, 3, 5.
b.   Represent the situation with a diagram or drawing.
c.   Explain or show how you know that each person will get the same amount of graham cracker.
Problem 2
Han’s work shows how 3 people could equally share 2 graham crackers.
###IMAGE0###
a.   How do you know that each person gets about the same amount of graham cracker? Explain or show your thinking. Organize it so it can be followed by others.
b.   Draw a diagram to show a different way that 3 people could share 2 graham crackers so each person got about the same amount of graham cracker.
Problem 3
a.   Using the set of cards (cut out from
Template: Equal Shares
), match each representation with a situation and expression. Some situations and expressions will have more than one matching representation.
b.   Choose one set of matched cards.
Show or explain how the diagram(s) and expression represent the number of sandwiches being shared.
Show or explain how the diagram(s) and expression represent the number of people sharing the sandwiches.
How much sandwich does each person get in the situation?

Task
Ben wrote a mixed number as the fraction 7$\frac{1}{3}$. Here is his work:
$$\begin{align}
7\frac{1}{3} &= \frac{7}{1}+\frac{1}{3} &\text{ (Step 1)} \\
&= \frac{(7\times 3)+1}{3} &\text{ (Step 2)} \\
&= \frac{21+1}{3} &\text{ (Step 3)} \\
&= \frac{22}{3} &\text{ (Step 4)}
\end{align}$$
Explain what Ben did in each step.

Problem 1
For each of the following, determine whether or not
$$\frac{2}{5}+\frac{3}{10}$$
represents the problem. Explain your decision.
a.   A farmer planted
$$\frac{2}{5}$$
of his forty acres in corn and another
$$\frac{3}{10}$$
of his land in wheat. Taken together, what fraction of the 40 acres had been planted in corn or wheat?
b.   Jim drank
$$\frac{2}{5}$$

$$\frac{3}{10}$$

c.   Allison has a batch of eggs in the incubator. On Monday

$$\frac{3}{10}$$
more of the original batch hatched. How many eggs hatched in all?
d.   Two fifths of the track team arrived at the weight room at 7 a.m. Ten minutes later,
$$\frac{3}{10}$$
of the team showed up. The rest of the team stayed home. What fraction of the team made it to the weight room that day?
e.   Andy made 2 free throws out of 5 free throw attempts. Jose made 3 free throws out of 10 free throw attempts. What is the fraction of free throw attempts that the two boys made together?
f.   Two fifths of the students in the fifth grade want to be in the band. Three tenths of the students in the fifth grade want to play in the orchestra. What fraction of the students in the fifth grade want to be in one of the two musical groups?
g.   Wesley ran
$$\frac{2}{5}$$
of a mile on Monday and
$$\frac{3}{10}$$
of a mile on Tuesday. How far did he run those two days?
h.   The fifth grade at Lincoln School has two soccer teams, Team A and Team B. If
$$\frac{2}{5}$$
of Team A are girls and
$$\frac{3}{10}$$
of Team B are girls, what fraction of the players from the two teams are girls?
i.   Two fifths of the students at Washington Elementary School like soccer best and
$$\frac{3}{10}$$
of them like basketball best. What fraction of students don’t like soccer or basketball best?

Problem 1
Ancient Egyptians used unit fractions, such as
$${{1\over2}}$$
and
$${{1\over3}}$$
, to represent all fractions. For example, they might write the number
$${{{{2\over3}}}}$$
as
$${{{1\over2}}}+{1\over6}$$
.
We often think of
$${{{{2\over3}}}}$$
as
$${{{1\over3}}}+{{{1\over3}}}$$
, but the ancient Egyptians would not write it this way because they didn’t use the same unit fraction twice.
a.   Write each of the following Egyptian fractions as a single fraction:
$${{{1\over2}}}+{{{1\over3}}}$$
$${{{1\over2}}}+{{{1\over3}}}+{1\over5}$$
$${{1\over4}+{1\over5}+{1\over12}}$$
b.   How might the ancient Egyptians have written the fraction we write as
$${{3\over4}}$$
?

$\maroonD{\dfrac{13}{10}}=\blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{10}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}} - \blueD2 \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{4} &\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}\\\\
-&\blueD{2}&\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}} -\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$.

We need to regroup $\blueD4\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$.



[[☃ image 1]]



$\blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}} =\blueD3 \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{4}}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD8 \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}} - \blueD1 \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.


$\begin{align}
&\blueD{8} &\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\\\
-&\blueD{1}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$.

We need to regroup $\blueD8\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} \blueD8\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}&=\blueD7+\maroonD{1}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\\\
&=\blueD7+ \maroonD{\dfrac44}+\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{4}}\\\\
&=\blueD7 \maroonD{\dfrac{6}{4}}
\end{align}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD5\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}} - \blueD3 \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{5}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.


$\begin{align}
&\blueD{5} &\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}}\\\\
-&\blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{5}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{5}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{5}}$.

We need to regroup $\blueD5\maroonD{ \dfrac{2}{5}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{5}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} \blueD5\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}}&=\blueD4+\maroonD{1}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}}\\\\
&=\blueD4+ \maroonD{\dfrac55}+\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{5}}\\\\
&=\blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}
\end{align}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD7 \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}} - \blueD5 \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.


$\begin{align}
&\blueD{7} &\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}}\\\\
-&\blueD{5}&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}$.

We need to regroup $\blueD7\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} \blueD7\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}}&=\blueD6+\maroonD{1}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}}\\\\
&=\blueD6+ \maroonD{\dfrac66}+\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{6}}\\\\
&=\blueD6 \maroonD{\dfrac{10}{6}}
\end{align}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD5 \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}} - \blueD3 \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}$
First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.


$\begin{align}
&\blueD{5} &\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}}\\\\
-&\blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}$.
We need to regroup $\blueD5\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} \blueD5\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}}&=\blueD4+\maroonD{1}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}}\\\\
&=\blueD4+ \maroonD{\dfrac88}+\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}}\\\\
&=\blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{12}{8}}
\end{align}$
We can replace $\blueD5 \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{8}}$ with $\blueD4 \maroonD{\dfrac{12}{8}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\blueD4}{\cancel{\blueD5}} &\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{12}{\cancel{4}}}{8}}\\\\
-&\,\ \blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}\\
\hline{}
\end{align}$
Now, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\blueD4}{\cancel{\blueD5}} &\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{12}{\cancel{4}}}{8}}\\\\
-&\,\ \blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}\\
\hline{}
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\end{align}$
Next, let's subtract the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\blueD4}{\cancel{\blueD5}} &\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{12}{\cancel{4}}}{8}}\\\\
-&\,\ \blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{8}}\\
\hline{}
&\,\ \blueD1&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\end{align}$
${1 \dfrac{5}{8}}={5 \dfrac{4}{8}} - {3\dfrac{7}{8}} $

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD{6} \maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}} - \blueD3\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{10}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.


$\begin{align}
&\blueD{6} &\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}\\\\
-&\blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{10}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{10}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{10}}$.

We need to regroup $\blueD{6}\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{10}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} \blueD6\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}&=\blueD5+\maroonD{1}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}\\\\
&=\blueD5+ \maroonD{\dfrac{10}{10}}+\maroonD{\dfrac{6}{10}}\\\\
&=\blueD5 \maroonD{\dfrac{16}{10}}
\end{align}$

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by subtracting each piece. 

$\blueD8 \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}} - \blueD3 \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$

First, let's subtract the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.


$\begin{align}
&\blueD{8} &\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}\\\\
-&\blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}\\
\hline\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$.

We need to regroup $\blueD8\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} \blueD8\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}&=\blueD7+\maroonD{1}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}\\\\
&=\blueD7+ \maroonD{\dfrac{8}{8}}+\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}\\\\
&=\blueD7 \maroonD{\dfrac{10}{8}}
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$2\dfrac{2}{5}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $\dfrac{34}{10}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{34}{10}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{34}{10}} &= \dfrac{10}{10}+\dfrac{10}{10}+\dfrac{10}{10} + \dfrac{4}{10} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+\dfrac{4}{10} \\  
\\
&=\green{3\dfrac{4}{10}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\blue{2\dfrac{2}{5}}$ and $\green{3\dfrac{4}{10}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

>$\blue{2} <\green{3}$

Because $\blue{2\dfrac{2}{5}}$ has a smaller whole number, we do not need to compare the fractional parts.

>$\blue{2\dfrac{2}{5}} <\green{3\dfrac{4}{10}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\blue{2\dfrac{2}{5}} < \green{\dfrac{34}{10}}$
